items.title: Overview of Traditional Mongolian Medical Warm Acupuncture.
items.summary: Mongolian medical warm acupuncture is a traditional therapy of Mongolian medicine and was developed by people living on the Mongolian Plateau. This kind of traditional oriental medicine has a long history. The main characteristics of Mongolian medical warm acupuncture are the acupoints and the...
items.content: Mongolian medical warm acupuncture is a traditional therapy of Mongolian medicine and was developed by people living on the Mongolian Plateau. This kind of traditional oriental medicine has a long history. The main characteristics of Mongolian medical warm acupuncture are the acupoints and the needles used. Its theory is based on the human anatomical structure and the distinct local culture. Mongolian medical warm acupuncture has been practiced for centuries and proved to be very effective in the treatment of age-related diseases, including the musculoskeletal and nervous diseases. This paper aims to briefly introduce the history and scope of Mongolian medical warm acupuncture, with a particular focus on age-related diseases, where Mongolian medical warm acupuncture has shown significant beneficial effects.
items.citation: Shao G, Xie W, Jia X, Bade R, Xie Y, Qi R, Gong K, Bai H, Si L, Chen Y, Sun K, Bo A. Overview of Traditional Mongolian Medical Warm Acupuncture. Aging and disease 13(4). (2022) (afiliacao: University of California, San Francisco) doi:10.14336/ad.2022.0115

items.title: Acupuncture for Chronic Low Back Pain in Older Adults: A Randomized Clinical Trial.
items.summary:
Importance

The study was carried out to inform Medicare acupuncture coverage decisions addressing the gap in evidence on acupuncture effectiveness, specifically for older adults with chronic low back pain (CLBP).

Objective

To determine the effectiveness of standard acupuncture (SA)...
items.content:
Importance

The study was carried out to inform Medicare acupuncture coverage decisions addressing the gap in evidence on acupuncture effectiveness, specifically for older adults with chronic low back pain (CLBP).

Objective

To determine the effectiveness of standard acupuncture (SA) or SA plus maintenance (enhanced acupuncture [EA]) to improve CLBP-related disability relative to usual medical care (UMC) at 3, 6, and 12 months after randomization.

Design, setting, and participants

This multisite, 3-arm, parallel-group randomized clinical trial of older adults with CLBP collected data from 4 US health care systems in 3 geographic areas and compared SA and EA treatment with UMC only. Study enrollment was conducted from August 12, 2021, to October 27, 2022; follow-up concluded on November 7, 2023.

Interventions

Both SA (8-15 treatment sessions over 12 weeks plus UMC) and EA (SA plus 4-6 maintenance sessions during the next 12 weeks) were provided by experienced, community-based licensed acupuncturists. Participants were randomized 1:1:1 to the 3 groups.

Main outcomes and measures

The primary outcome was CLBP-related disability measured by a baseline-to-6-month change in the Roland-Morris Disability Questionnaire (RMDQ) score. Secondary outcomes included pain intensity and the percentage of participants with clinically meaningful (≥30%) improvements.

Results

The trial identified 800 individuals who were randomized to 3 groups (mean [SD] age, 73.6 [6.0] years; 496 females [62.0%]). At 6 months, RMDQ change scores were significantly better in both the SA and EA groups compared with the UMC only group (SA vs UMC: adjusted mean difference, -1.0 [95% CI, -1.9 to -0.1] and EA vs UMC: adjusted mean difference, -1.5 [95% CI, -2.5 to -0.6]). SA and EA change scores did not differ significantly from one another. The relative benefit of acupuncture compared with UMC on disability persisted at 12 months. Pain intensity exhibited a relative benefit of EA over SA at 6 months, and both acupuncture groups had significant improvement over UMC. The adjusted percentage with clinically meaningful improvements in RMDQ at 6 months was greater for SA (39.1% [95% CI, 33.1%-46.1%]; adjusted relative risk, 1.33 [95% CI, 1.04-1.70]) and for EA (43.8% [95% CI, 38.0%-50.4%]; adjusted relative risk, 1.49 [95% CI, 1.19-1.86]) compared with UMC (29.4% [95% CI, 24.3%-35.5%]) and persisted at 12 months. Rates of serious adverse events were low and similar among groups, with less than 1% that was possibly acupuncture-intervention related.

Conclusions and relevance

The findings of this randomized clinical trial of older adults with CLBP suggest that acupuncture needling provided greater improvements in back pain-related disability at 6 months and at 12 months compared with UMC alone. These findings support acupuncture needling as an effective and safe treatment option for older adults with CLBP.

Trial registration

ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT04982315.
items.citation: DeBar LL, Wellman RD, Justice M, Avins AL, Beyrouty M, Eng CM, Herman PM, Nielsen A, Pressman A, Stone KL, Teets RY, Cook AJ. Acupuncture for Chronic Low Back Pain in Older Adults: A Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA network open 8(9). (2025) (afiliacao: University of California, San Francisco) doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2025.31348

items.title: Comparing the Effect of Adding Acupuncture and Pain Counseling to Usual Care on Reducing Pain among Patients with Cancer – The PRACXIS Study
items.summary:
Background

At least half of hospitalized patients with cancer experience pain during their stay. Using nonpharmacologic treatments alongside medication may better address patients' total pain experience by relieving physical and psychological symptoms and reducing use of analgesics....
items.content:
Background

At least half of hospitalized patients with cancer experience pain during their stay. Using nonpharmacologic treatments alongside medication may better address patients' total pain experience by relieving physical and psychological symptoms and reducing use of analgesics. Building on evidence supporting acupuncture and pain counseling, we evaluated these therapies in hospitals serving diverse populations.

Objectives

The aims of Pragmatic Research of Acupuncture and pain Counseling eXtended to Inpatient Services (PRACXIS) were to determine the effectiveness of adjunctive acupuncture and pain counseling for improving pain management (aim 1), determine whether adjunctive acupuncture and pain counseling reduce the use of opioid analgesics and side effects (aim 2), and examine stakeholder perspectives on nonpharmacologic pain management (aim 3).

Methods

We conducted a hybrid effectiveness-implementation trial of adjunctive acupuncture and pain counseling using a 2×2 factorial design. Adults hospitalized for an anticipated stay of 48 hours or longer with a malignant solid tumor and moderate to severe pain were eligible for the trial. Participants received usual care and were randomized to acupuncture, pain counseling, neither, or both. The study was conducted in Cantonese, English, and Spanish at a university-based tertiary-care hospital and a public safety-net hospital. Participants randomized to a study intervention were offered acupuncture and/or pain counseling once daily for up to 4 hospital days. Patient-reported outcomes were collected during 5 hospital days (baseline and 4 follow-up surveys); medication data were extracted from electronic health records for the duration of patients' hospital stay; exploratory surveys were collected 1 and 3 months after hospital discharge. Our primary outcomes for aims 1 and 2 were change in daily worst pain intensity, measured on an 11-point numeric rating scale (0-10), and change in daily use of opioid medications. We analyzed changes in patient-reported, pain-related outcomes and opioid medications using linear mixed models with intention-to-treat (ITT) and per-protocol analysis. We tested for main effects of acupuncture vs no acupuncture, pain counseling vs no pain counseling, and intervention effects in the presence and absence of one another (acupuncture only, pain counseling only, acupuncture plus pain counseling, usual care). We also conducted in-depth qualitative interviews with stakeholders on patient preferences, barriers, and facilitators of nonpharmacologic pain management in hospitals. We used inductive and deductive coding to analyze qualitative interviews.

Results

Participants (N = 447; mean age, 59 years) were 49% women, 10% Asian, 7% Black, 25% Latino, and 52% White; 78% had nonmetastatic cancer and 33% had gastrointestinal cancers. Average worst pain intensity at baseline was 7.6. In ITT analysis, based on randomized assignment and adjusted for covariates, between-group differences of main effects of acupuncture vs no acupuncture and pain counseling vs no pain counseling were nonsignificant. The primary outcome—change in worst pain intensity, measured on a 0-to-10 numeric rating scale—was −0.63 per day (95% CI, −0.79 to −0.46) in the acupuncture group vs −0.49 (95% CI, −0.66 to −0.32) for no acupuncture; −0.56 (95% CI, −0.73 to −0.39) in the pain counseling group vs −0.56 (95% CI, −0.73 to −0.39) for no pain counseling. No significant differences were observed for randomization to acupuncture or to pain counseling for pain relief, health-related quality of life measures, or emotional distress. In adjusted ITT models for the co-primary outcome—change in opioid daily dose—group differences for oral morphine equivalent were not statistically significant; between-group differences were −1.80 mg/day (95% CI, −6.05 to 2.46) for acupuncture vs no acupuncture and −2.83 (95% CI, −7.14 to 1.49) for pain counseling vs no pain counseling. Based on evidence of potential interaction between the study interventions, we also assessed outcomes by the 4 treatment arms based on ITT. Worst pain decreased by 0.73 for acupuncture, 0.59 for pain counseling, 0.54 for acupuncture plus pain counseling, and 0.40 for usual care. The 0.33 between-group difference of acupuncture vs usual care was not statistically significant (P = .056), nor were the other comparisons with usual care. The opioid morphine equivalent decreased 4.16 mg/day (95% CI, 0.08-8.24) among inpatients who received acupuncture plus pain counseling vs 0.33 mg (95% CI, −4.06 to 4.71) among inpatients in usual care, a nonsignificant between-group difference (P = .14). A substantial portion of patients (41%) who were randomly assigned to study interventions did not receive them. We therefore conducted a per-protocol analysis to examine intervention effects based on actual treatment receipt. In per-protocol analysis, worst pain decreased by 0.81 per day (95% CI, 0.53-1.09) in patients who received acupuncture compared with 0.40 (95% CI, 0.15-0.64) in patients who received usual care (P = .03). Pain counseling was associated with the greatest improvement in emotional distress, symptom burden, and perceived pain relief, but differences were statistically not significant compared with other arms. Qualitative data (n = 86) indicated patient satisfaction with acupuncture and pain counseling, highlighted the value of biopsychosocial approaches in hospital-based pain management for stakeholders, and discussed models to improve access to nonpharmacologic therapies.

Conclusions

Nonpharmacologic approaches offered with usual care may improve hospital-based pain management for diverse inpatients with cancer. The evidence is difficult to interpret, however, owing largely to a 41% rate of failure to receive the randomized study intervention. We did not find effects of acupuncture or pain counseling based on ITT analyses. In per-protocol analysis by treatment arm, acupuncture was associated with greater improvements in worst pain intensity than usual care alone. Acupuncture plus pain counseling was associated with greater reductions in opioid consumption than usual care alone. Pain counseling may improve psychological outcomes related to pain, such as emotional distress. Optimal pain management should include a multicomponent strategy aligned with clinical presentation, patient priorities, and preferences.

Limitations

Our practice-based research included bias from patients who did not receive an intervention, missing outcome data, and lack of statistical power for a comparison among the 4 treatment arms. The ITT and per-protocol analyses reflect differences between random assignment and treatment receipt. The per-protocol analyses were based on a substantially reduced sample size.
items.citation: Chao MT, Reddy S, Hartogensis W, Abrams DI, Dhruva A, Ho EY, Thompson-Lastad A, Thompson N, Leung I, Ashby J, Hullender Rubin L, Santana T, Toveg M, Ruvalcaba D, Ritter V, Turchin C, Ye H, Trejo E, Cheng J, Hecht FM. Comparing the Effect of Adding Acupuncture and Pain Counseling to Usual Care on Reducing Pain among Patients with Cancer – The PRACXIS Study . (2023) (afiliacao: University of California, San Francisco)

items.title: A Survey of Licensed Acupuncturists in the San Francisco Bay Area: Prevalence of Treating Oncology Patients.
items.summary:
Background

Many cancer patients seek traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), the prevalence varying with diagnosis, comorbidities, and demographics. Interventions sought include acupuncture, massage, herbs, diet, and exercise, usually combined with conventional therapies. It is not known what...
items.content:
Background

Many cancer patients seek traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), the prevalence varying with diagnosis, comorbidities, and demographics. Interventions sought include acupuncture, massage, herbs, diet, and exercise, usually combined with conventional therapies. It is not known what proportion of TCM practitioners care for cancer patients, their cancer specific training or caseload, what interventions they employ, their outcomes, and their communication patterns with conventional oncologists.

Methods

A survey was mailed to all 2213 licensed acupuncturists in the 9-county San Francisco Bay Area gathering descriptive statistics.

Results

A total of 472 (21%) responded by mail or web-based Research Electronic Data Capture (REDCap) tool. Most respondents (77%) reported caring for patients with cancer, with 29% reporting having 6 to 10 years of practice experience, and 44.2% having 0 to 20 hours of training specific to the needs of patients with cancer. Improving quality of life was reported by 94% as what their treatment offered cancer patients as well as the area where treatment was felt to have the greatest impact. The most useful TCM modalities were acupuncture (98%), herbs (79%), diet (72%), moxibustion (46%), and meditation instruction (44%). Absence of adverse reactions was noted by 95%. Ninety-one percent reported "never" or "hardly ever" having been contacted by patients' oncologists to discuss treatment.

Conclusions

Many acupuncturists seeing cancer patients have significant clinical experience and have sought specialized training. Improved communication is needed between TCM practitioners and oncologists sharing care of cancer patients.
items.citation: Abrams D, McCulloch M, Cohen M, Liaw M, Silverman D, Wilson C. A Survey of Licensed Acupuncturists in the San Francisco Bay Area: Prevalence of Treating Oncology Patients. Integrative cancer therapies 17(1). (2018) (afiliacao: University of California, San Francisco) doi:10.1177/1534735416684946

items.title: The Adjunctive Effect of Acupuncture for Advanced Cancer Patients in a Collaborative Model of Palliative Care: Study Protocol for a 3-Arm Randomized Trial.
items.summary:
Background

Cancer is the second leading cause of death before the age of 70. Improved cancer survival has put increasing demands on cancer care. Palliative care is the specialized multi-disciplinary care providing relief from the pain, symptoms, and stress of serious illness. The study aims...
items.content:
Background

Cancer is the second leading cause of death before the age of 70. Improved cancer survival has put increasing demands on cancer care. Palliative care is the specialized multi-disciplinary care providing relief from the pain, symptoms, and stress of serious illness. The study aims to evaluate the adjunctive effect of acupuncture for advanced cancer patients in a collaborative model of palliative care.

Methods/design

This is a single-blinded, randomized, sham-controlled trial. One hundred twenty advanced cancer patients undergoing palliative care will be randomized in a ratio of 2:1:1 to manual acupuncture plus standard care group (ASC), sham acupuncture plus standard care group (SSC), and standard care group (SC). Patients in ASC and SSC will receive 9 sessions of acupuncture or sham acupuncture for 3 weeks, and will be followed up for 2 months. The primary measure is the change from baseline score of the Edmonton Symptom Assessment System at 3 weeks. The secondary measures include the Brief Fatigue Inventory, Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale, Insomnia Severity Index, Numeric Rating Scale, and European Organization for Research and Treatment of Cancer Quality of Life 15 items Questionnaire for Palliative Care.

Discussion

The finding of this trial will provide high-quality evidence on the adjunctive effect of acupuncture to standard care on advanced cancer patients undergoing palliative care.

Trial registration

Clinicaltrials.gov, NCT04398875 (https://www.clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT04398875), Registered on 21 May 2020.
items.citation: Chen H, So TH, Cho WC, Qin Z, Ma CH, Li SG, Yang Z, Jiang F, Wu J, Zhang ZJ, Kong FM, Lao L. The Adjunctive Effect of Acupuncture for Advanced Cancer Patients in a Collaborative Model of Palliative Care: Study Protocol for a 3-Arm Randomized Trial. Integrative cancer therapies 20. (2021) (afiliacao: University of California, San Francisco) doi:10.1177/15347354211012749
